---
title: "Python Packages 1 : Setup"
author: "nshephard"
date: "2022-11-30"
categories: [code, analysis, python, packages, packaging, git, github, gitlab]
image: https://live.staticflickr.com/65535/52433320790_70a4ce0bf4_k.jpg
---

This is the first in a series of posts aims to describe the basics of setting up, configuring, documenting, and
publishing a Python package, incorporating some good practices such as licensing, contributing guidelines, automated
linting and testing both locally and using CI/CD (Continuous Integration/Development),

I dislike using the term "_Best Practice_" to describe what I'm advocating and prefer describing it as  "_Good
Practice_" because I know what I'm doing isn't perfect. I welcome
[feedback](https://github.com/ns-rse/ns-rse.github.io/issues) if you've spotted an error (no matter how small, even
typos) , think I've missed out something, or could improve somewhere, or if you just think I'm plain wrong, please do
get in touch and let me know.

There are many introductions to [Python]() but this series of posts is not meant to be one of them. Rather it is assumed you
have some familiarity with writing Python code and know some basics such that it is an interpreted language rather than
one that requires a compiling. You will likely have written some code and are wondering how to structure it into a
package that you can make available to others. Further it is assumed you are familiar with using [Git](https://git.vc)
and online Forge's such as [GitHub](https://www.github.com)/[GitLab](https://gitlab.com)/etc.


# Initialising a Repository

The simplest way to start a repository is to initialise it on GitHub/GitLab and then clone it locally. If you already
have a directory under version control locally you should add an `origin` so that you have a remote back-up and make
your code Open Source and available to others to work on and collaborate with.

## Key Files

### `README.md`


### `LICENSE.md`

### `CONTRIBUTING.md`

# Python Package Structure

## Configuration Files

## Modules/Sub-Modules

## Documentation

## Tests
